data.title: What The Smartest Canadian Investors Hold In Their Dividend Growth Portfolios
data.description:
Every year, Dividend Stocks Rock compiles a unique list: the most widely held stocks by its PRO members. With over 2,600 portfolios contributing to this list, it’s more than a popularity contest—it’s a reflection of what experienced, long-term dividend growth investors trust to deliver both income and resilience. This post spotlights three of Canada’s most-owned dividend stocks, offering insight into what seasoned investors value today and what DSR bull and bear cases are.

The U.S. companies will follow up next week.

Fortis (FTS:CA), (FTS) – The Boring Backbone of Stability Investors Count On

Fortis keeps on getting more love year after year. Fortis is a classic utility offering its customers transmission and distribution of electricity and natural gas. The company likes to remind investors that Fortis is virtually 100% regulated. It’s a symbol of stability and predictable cash flow.

Why it’s in the top 5

Fortis isn’t flashy. It’s reliable. It operates almost entirely regulated utilities across 18 jurisdictions.

What seasoned investors like

  • 50 years of uninterrupted dividend growth.

  • Recession-resistant and rate-regulated, it’s the poster child for “sleep-well-at-night” investing.

DSR Bull Case

Fortis is a top-tier regulated utility with operations across Canada, the U.S., and the Caribbean, generating 99% of its revenue from stable, government-approved sources. Its C$26 billion capital plan aims to grow its rate base by 6.5% annually through 2028, supporting steady earnings and dividend increases. With 50 consecutive years of dividend growth and growing investments in transmission and renewable energy, Fortis offers a rare blend of income reliability and long-term infrastructure-driven growth. It’s a classic “buy and hold” stock for conservative, income-focused investors.

DSR Bear Case

Despite its stability, Fortis faces headwinds from its capital-intensive business model and reliance on regulatory approvals. With one-third of its investment plan funded by debt, rising interest rates could pressure earnings and limit flexibility. Regulatory setbacks or environmental policy shifts may further strain growth. Currency fluctuations also pose a risk, as 60% of revenue is U.S.-based. In a competitive utility landscape, rivals with leaner operations or faster-growing assets could outpace Fortis in total returns.

Its portfolio role

Defensive core holding—ideal during volatile markets and for steady dividend growth.

Royal Bank (RY:CA), (RY) – Canada’s Financial Fortress with Global Reach

Royal Bank is the largest Canadian Bank by market cap and offers a wide variety of products and services. The business is separated into several segments, including Personal & Commercial (P&C), Capital Markets, Wealth Management, and Insurance.

Why it’s in the top 5

Royal Bank is a bedrock of stability, with diversification across personal banking, capital markets, and wealth management.

What seasoned investors like

  • Diversified revenue streams protect against housing market weakness.

  • Strong dividend growth track record and capital strength.

DSR Bull Case

Royal Bank of Canada stands as the country’s largest bank, supported by a well-diversified business model across banking, wealth management, insurance, and capital markets. Over half its revenue now comes from non-traditional banking sectors, offering stability and profitability beyond interest margins. Its international expansion adds resilience against domestic economic slowdowns, while strong brand recognition and regulatory protection create high barriers to entry. With steady earnings, growing fee-based revenue, and a reliable dividend history, RBC is a core holding for investors seeking both income and long-term growth.

DSR Bear Case

Despite its diversification, RBC remains vulnerable to macroeconomic pressures, particularly Canada’s housing market. Rising interest rates and economic uncertainty could lead to higher mortgage defaults and elevated credit losses. The bank has already increased provisions in anticipation of a downturn. Regulatory constraints limit growth flexibility, while a potential recession could dampen lending and capital markets activity. Competition from other major banks and global players also pressures RBC to continuously innovate just to maintain its position. These challenges may weigh on profitability and limit upside in the near term.

Its portfolio role

Anchor stock for both income and growth investors.

Enbridge (ENB:CA), (ENB) – Reliable Income from a Pipeline Powerhouse

Enbridge lost some love this year and was beaten out by Royal Bank. Some investors would be right to highlight the amazing year ENB had on the market. A part of that “bull run” is explained by its poor performance last year. Unfortunately, this is often the case for such a company: ENB stock price is known to go up and down through cycles. I bought it during one of those down cycles and was happy to sell it at a high price before the 2023 drop.

Why it’s one of the most held stocks

Enbridge combines reliable income with defensive characteristics, making it ideal for retirees and income investors.

What seasoned investors like

  • Enbridge operates one of North America’s largest pipeline systems, generating toll-like income from oil and gas transportation.

  • The company has increased its dividend for 29 consecutive years—a rare feat in the energy space.

  • Investors appreciate its strategic pivot toward natural gas and renewables, improving its long-term sustainability.

DSR Bull Case

Enbridge is a North American energy infrastructure giant, offering stable, inflation-resistant cash flow through long-term, take-or-pay contracts. Its Mainline system handles 70% of Canada’s crude exports, while recent acquisitions have expanded its footprint in natural gas transmission—now half its business. The company is targeting 7–9% annual EBITDA growth through 2026 and is investing in energy transition projects like renewable natural gas and offshore wind. With hard-to-replicate assets and a growing utility base, Enbridge remains a reliable income-generating powerhouse for long-term dividend investors.

DSR Bear Case

Enbridge’s aggressive growth strategy has led to a soaring debt load—reaching nearly $100 billion in 2024—which could strain future investments and dividend growth, especially amid rising interest rates and inflation. Regulatory and environmental hurdles remain a persistent threat, with pipeline projects often delayed or derailed. Increasing carbon taxes and operating costs in Canada may further erode margins. While Enbridge’s asset base is robust, its debt-heavy model puts it at a disadvantage compared to leaner midstream competitors with stronger balance sheets.

Its role in a portfolio

Enbridge is your premium income generator. It likely won’t deliver much capital growth, but it offers a steady, well-covered yield and solid inflation-beating income.

Final Thoughts: Seasoned Investors Choose Balance

DSR PRO members aren’t just picking high yielders or tech flyers. They’re selecting companies that combine resilience, income, and future potential. That’s a powerful takeaway for anyone building or reviewing a dividend portfolio.

Portfolio Role Recap Table

Stock Role in Portfolio Ideal For
Fortis Defensive Core Stability, retirement income
Royal Bank Balanced Anchor Growth + income blend
Enbridge Yield Generator Income-focused investors

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Krispy Kreme: Avoid These Stale Doughnuts
data.description:
It's been a while since I last reviewed the operations of Krispy Kreme, Inc. (NASDAQ:DNUT). In my last article, I was cautious about Krispy Kreme's prospects, as the company was trading at elevated valuation multiples with a lack of profitability and earnings.

Since my article, Krispy Kreme's shares have plummeted by over 60% (not counting the stock's pre-market plunge on May 8th, 2025) while the rest of the markets have soared (Figure 1). The market has certainly vindicated my cautious stance.

However, as I sit in my office enjoying a Krispy Kreme donut and coffee (my local Costco had them for sale at $9.99 for 15), I wonder if DNUT's share prices have fallen to attractive levels given the recent plunge?

Unfortunately, looking at the details of the business, Krispy Kreme appears to be worse off than before. The stock's valuation is now 79.7x Fwd P/E compared to 43.6x previously, and the company's recent partnership with McDonald's appears to be a dud.

With nearly $1 billion in debt and little cash, there are serious solvency concerns at Krispy Kreme. I continue to recommend investors stay away from owning shares of this doughnut maker.

Brief Company Overview

First, for those not familiar, Krispy Kreme Inc. sells its iconic donuts through donut shops, kiosks, and ecommerce delivery across the United States and the world. Krispy Kreme has one of the world's most recognizable brands, with over 100 billion media impressions in 2024 (Figure 2).

2024 Was A Transformational Year

Going over the company's recent history, 2024 was a transformational year for Krispy Kreme. First, as mentioned in my previous article, Krispy Kreme partnered with McDonald's (MCD) to trial its donuts in ~160 McDonald's restaurants.

The trial was successful, and two companies formalized a partnership agreement to roll out Krispy Kreme donuts to all McDonald's U.S. restaurants in a phased approach until the end of 2026. There are over 13,000 McDonald's restaurants in the U.S., and as of March 30, 2025, Krispy Kreme donuts were available in over 2,400 of them.

Furthermore, in July 2024, Krispy Kreme also divested its majority stake in Insomnia Cookies to a private equity firm, Verlinvest and Mistral Equity Partners, for $350 million in enterprise value. This represented double the valuation at which Krispy Kreme invested in 2018, and provided Krispy Kreme with a much-needed cash infusion.

The Insomnia transaction closed on July 17, 2024, and Krispy Kreme received ~$170 million in cash that the company used to pay down its heavy debt load. Krispy Kreme continues to hold a 34% minority stake in Insomnia Cookies.

All told, the company's strategic actions were aimed at improving profitability in its U.S. business while simultaneously deleveraging its balance sheet (Figure 2).

String Of Bad Misses Reduced Management Credibility

Unfortunately, the company has had a string of bad operating and financial results that have reduced investors' confidence in the management team and their vision. First, Krispy Kreme missed Q3/24 earnings badly, with an operating loss of $0.01 / share versus $0.03 / share in operating profit in Q3/23. The company also had to lower its FY2024 guidance on the back of poor operating results.

Then, in December, Krispy Kreme disclosed its IT systems were hacked, disrupting its online ordering systems. Ultimately, the IT hack resulted in ~$11 million in lost sales and was a big contributor to the company's weak Q4/24 earnings of just a penny in Q4/24 earnings compared to $0.09 in Q4/23.

Krispy Kreme's 2025 guidance also disappointed investors, as the company expected sales of $1.55 to $1.65 billion, far below the consensus estimate at the time of $1.78 billion and a decline YoY compared to $1.67 billion in FY2024.

Q1/25 The Latest Straw

Krispy Kreme's bad luck / bad execution has carried over into 2025, as the company reported abysmal Q1/25 results. For Q1/25, Krispy Kreme reported a 15% YoY decline in sales to $375 million, and a plunge to an operating loss of $20 million (Figure 3).

While the reduction in revenues was largely expected due to the de-consolidation of Insomnia sales, Krispy Kreme saw a worrisome 1.0% decline in organic growth, as the company blamed "consumer softness leading to a decline in doughnut shop transaction volume" (Figure 4).

The decline in U.S. sales was particularly puzzling, as the company had added thousands of access points via its McDonald's partnership (Figure 5). Krispy Kreme's organic growth was -2.6% YoY in the first quarter.

Are consumers forgoing their guilty pleasures due to Trump's tariff uncertainty, or are there other factors at play? Perhaps the opening of more retail doors oversaturated the market and cannibalized sales at existing locations? Management was very evasive on the earnings call and did not give a clear answer as to why organic sales declined in the U.S.

International sales were better, as the International segment saw 1.5% YoY organic growth, led by sales in Canadian Costco stores (yes, I'm one of those customers!) (Figure 6).

Pulls Guidance, Cuts Dividend, And Hunkers Down

As a result of the poor quarterly results and "macro uncertainty", Krispy Kreme also decided to suspend its full-year guidance for 2025, barely 3 months after first proposing the guidance following Q4 results (Figure 7).

In addition, Krispy Kreme also suspended its quarterly dividend and will redirect dividend payments to reduce its debt load.

Slows Down McDonald's Expansion

In a quarter of disappointing news, the most disappointing has to be the company's decision NOT to expand the number of McDonald's restaurants offering Krispy Kreme donuts in the second quarter. In my opinion, this was the primary reason DNUT's share price plunged more than 20% on May 8th.

Recall, I cautioned against the investor euphoria when the McDonald's partnership was first announced. For Krispy Kreme to now announce a pause in the multi-year rollout, less than a year into the partnership, is a sign that executives at both Krispy Kreme and McDonald's did not have a good grasp of consumer demand and tastes on a national level.

While it was not specifically called out, reading between the lines of management's earnings call responses, I believe the main issue is that after an initial "honeymoon" period, consumers were not buying doughnuts in McDonald's restaurants at the same rate as Krispy Kreme expected in its expansion plans.

Debt Is A Big Overhang

Looking forward, a big overhang on Krispy Kreme is its gargantuan debt load. With nearly $1 billion in long-term debt, Krispy Kreme's net leverage ratio is now 6.1x, as debt increased QoQ and adj. EBITDA declined (Figure 8).

It is unclear if this constitutes a default under the company's debt covenants, which stipulate a maximum net leverage ratio of 5.0x (Figure 9).

Valuation In The Dumps?

After DNUT's 60%+ stock decline, how does the company's valuation look? Unfortunately, even after the stock's decline, Krispy Kreme's valuation is even more expensive, as earnings have collapsed (Figure 10). Krispy Kreme is now trading at 79.7x Fwd P/E, compared to 43.6x previously.

On Fwd EV/EBITDA, DNUT is now trading at 11.4x compared to 17.9x previously.

Risk

The biggest risk to DNUT remains the discretionary nature of its products. As was mentioned by management, consumer sentiment is terrible, with tariff uncertainty causing consumers to pull back on discretionary spending.

On the other hand, a dozen donuts could be an affordable luxury that consumers will continue to indulge in (e.g., a lipstick effect), despite poor economic prospects.

Another risk with DNUT is the 'unhealthy' image of doughnuts in general. With many obese consumers going on GLP1 treatments to manage their weight and appetites, perhaps that is having a negative effect on demand for unhealthy foods like doughnuts.

Conclusion

While investors may be tempted to buy some shares of DNUT, as the doughnut maker is trading at all-time lows, I believe the company's future remains highly uncertain. The recently announced partnership with McDonald's appears dead on arrival ("DOA") as barely a year into the partnership, Krispy Kreme is already pausing its rollout plans due to poor economics.

I remain cautious on DNUT shares and recommend investors stay away.

data.title: Powell Industries Q2: A Great Buying Opportunity For Longs
data.description:
Introduction

One of my largest holdings, Powell Industries, Inc. (POWL), reported what I consider a very good quarter, which was met rather negatively by the investing community, which overlooked a massive beat on earnings by focusing on slightly slowed new orders. I wanted to go through the numbers and give my thoughts on the company’s prospects and why I think this is a great buying opportunity.

By The Numbers

Let’s look at the top-line performance. Sales came in at $279m, +9.2% y/y, but slightly missed analysts’ estimates by around $4m. Q2 GAAP EPS came in at $3.81, which beat estimates by a decent margin of 0.37 cents.

Looking at the revenue breakdown in a bit more detail, we can see which segments performed the best. Oil and Gas excl. Petrochemicals still lead the way in terms of total revenues in percentage terms, showing a little weakness this time around, declining 2.5% y/y, as well as petrochemicals with a -12% decline y/y. Electric utility has been the key revenue driver for the company, with a whopping 48% growth y/y, while Commercial and Industrial grew at around 16% y/y. The rest are not as impressive in dollar terms, but Light rail traction power grew 125% y/y to $10m.

As I said, a clear standout here was the Electric Utility segment. The surge was primarily driven by increased project volume and strong demand in the sector, with notable wins including a major contract for a new greenfield LNG facility on the U.S. Gulf Coast and a mining project in Canada, which also helped the Commercial and Industrial segment.

Moving on to efficiency and profitability, another surprise was the company’s operational efficiency improvements. The company’s gross margins significantly improved compared to last year, by an impressive 530bps y/y to around 29.9%. That is also over 500 bps sequential improvement. The disciplined project execution and closeouts helped the company’s margins significantly in the past quarter, as well as operational efficiency across the business. Strong volume leverage was mentioned as a key contributor to the margin profile. Net Income saw growth of 38% y/y, as well as EPS.

New orders were up 6% y/y, but down 7% sequentially, which may have spooked some investors, as it looks like there may be a shift in the overall demand; however, it is too early to tell if that is the case. It may be just a fluke. The next couple of quarters will tell us a bit more about that. Backlog has been steady at $1.3B, which may be perceived as negative since the same number has been around for many quarters now. It is, however, providing visibility into FY2027, and such visibility is always welcome.

Looking at the company’s financial position, it is still as good as it was the last time I covered it. Even better, actually, since the company’s position improved by around $30m, and it still has no debt on its books. The company’s balance sheet is very strong, which supports its future growth quite well. All the capital can be focused on expanding the company’s footprint and gaining more market share.

Overall, the report was great in my opinion, which unfortunately was overshadowed by the slight decline in new orders and steady backlog, which was probably seen as a negative, as well as a slight miss on revenue expectations. The massive beat on earnings took a backseat because of it, and I think that presents an opportunity for the long-term investors.

Last quarter, the big reason the company’s share price went down on the day was the deteriorated gross margin, as everyone thought the company was losing its profitability. Fast forward to three months later, the margin worries are not there any longer, but investors found a new reason to bring this company down. And to be honest, I’m grateful because it allows me to accumulate further.

Comments on the Outlook

So, let’s talk about margins. Should we expect the current gross margin of 29.9% to be the new norm going forward? No. I expect it to come down slightly over the remainder of the year, unless the company manages to secure a lot more high-margin contracts, which is not unheard of. Prime example Q2 results. The company’s CFO, Mike Metcalf, said during the earnings call that the margin profile should “align with the levels reported in the first half of the year.” So, if we look at the margin profile for the first half reported, we come to 27.5% gross margin. This is what the company expects its margin profile to look like at the end of the year, which is still better than the first quarter by around 250bps. The company is still trending in the right direction here, and I expect further operational efficiency improvements over the long term.

The company’s backlog has been steady at $1.3B for a while now, which many perceive as negative. I don’t agree with that sentiment at all. A steady backlog is a lot better than a declining one. The company hasn’t experienced a slowdown in backlog as of yet, which tells me there is still a lot of demand for new projects in many different sectors. I mentioned earlier, the backlog provides a solid revenue visibility for investors into 2027 now, which helps us investors see that the services of POWL are in high demand.

With the facility expansion in Houston, I can see that the backlog, if the demand remains as high as it is now, will start to increase going forward. There may have been capacity constraints that stopped the company from adding more contracts in the future, but now that may change over the next few quarters. At least that is what I think.

Furthermore, the company is increasing its R&D investment to launch new products, like a new low-voltage switchgear that has been designed for data centers. Data center capex by big tech is going strong. All the big tech companies recently reported their numbers, and most of them either reported the same capex spend for 2025 or increased it. Meta Platforms (META) raised its full-year 2025 capex guidance, Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG) maintained, while Microsoft (MSFT) decreased it slightly. The company is also manufacturing new products like the new power control aisle substation, which is ramping up, and is expected to contribute to revenue generation in the second half of 2025.

The management has also said that they are focusing on expanding in its three “home geographies,” the U.S., Canada, and the UK, which means further offshore investments are not out of the question and may contribute positively to the backlog in the future.

I still believe the LNG sector, along with the electric utility, will be the key growth drivers for the next year if everything goes well in the macroeconomic sense.

Tariff worries are still there. When analysts asked the management about the tariff situation and its impact, as well as other commercial risks surrounding the new LNG projects and prices on the company, CEO Brett Cope said that there are still a lot of unknowns regarding the situation on the global economies. However, he emphasized that, based on conversations with the company’s clients regarding the recent projects awarded as well as the potential future projects, the outlook is robust. Despite the negativity in the headlines, POWL’s clients are going ahead with their projects, which is a very positive sign for the company. No cancellations or delays have been mentioned.

Valuation

I went ahead and updated the discounted cash flow ("DCF") model with the assumptions that may be a bit more conservative, as well as adding assumptions from the management and what they expect the company’s operations to do over the longer term.

For revenues, I kept a similar profile to before, meaning over the next decade, the company’s sales will grow at only 7% CAGR, which is very achievable in my view.

The next part is where I changed the assumptions. Since the company doesn’t expect gross margins to be as robust as they have been this past quarter, I decided to lower them to around 27.5% for FY26 and FY27. After that, I went ahead and improved them slightly for the remainder of the model because with further expansion and focusing on high-margin contracts, it is very possible to achieve 29% gross margins by FY34.

For my discount, I am using the company’s WACC of 7.68% and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. And to keep it a bit more conservative, I went with an additional 15% discount on the final intrinsic value of POWL. With that said, the company is trading at quite a discount to its fair value of $312 a share.

Closing Comments

I don’t think Powell Industries, Inc. will remain at such low levels for much longer. The tariff worries are certainly playing a huge role currently; however, once that passes, I can see the company shooting back up to over $250 a share or even higher. Even at such profitability levels and single-digit revenue growth, the company is a buy at these prices. I purchased more when it went to around $170 recently, and I will not hesitate to buy even more if it drops further. The company is a steal at these prices in my opinion.

data.title: Long Runway Of Steady Growth In Korea For Coupang
data.description:
Coupang: Fulfillment Is Strategy

Coupang (NYSE:CPNG) is the undisputed leader in Korea’s e-commerce market. Most other players — including 11st, Gmarket, SSG.COM (these three saw sales fall 28.9%, 16.4% and 6.2% respectively YoY) — have fallen behind, unable to compete with Coupang’s virtuous flywheel. Today, only Naver and AliExpress (BABA) remain as significant challengers, and even they are falling behind in different ways.

What sets Coupang apart is simple but hard to replicate: control over fulfillment - it has spent more than $5 billion building out its logistic network in Korea. Unlike marketplace platforms that act as intermediaries, Coupang owns its entire stack — inventory (for 1P business), warehouses, and delivery fleet. This gives it unmatched speed, reliability, and consistency. Korean consumers value convenience deeply, and Coupang delivers next-day (often same-day) across vast SKUs — a game-changer.

Owning the fulfillment network has also allowed Coupang to turbo-charge its nascent but fast-growing 3P business - an important growth driver. It offers sellers a plug-and-play logistics solution through FLC (Fulfillment and Logistics by Coupang). This creates a powerful incentive for merchants to join Coupang's marketplace. Instead of struggling with fragmented shipping or inconsistent delivery experiences, they can rely on Coupang’s network to handle fulfillment with the same speed and reliability customers expect from Rocket Delivery.

This quarter, we saw strong momentum in FLC with sellers, selection, and overall volumes all growing at multiples of our overall business... it has quickly become a tremendous growth engine for sellers who want to tap into the speed, convenience, and efficiencies we offer. By handling storage, packing, shipping, and even returns for our third party merchants, FLC dramatically lowers the barriers to success for tens of thousands of small businesses.

Q1 2025 Earnings Transcript

As more sellers choose Coupang, Coupang’s marketplace also gains breadth and depth, giving buyers more choice while maintaining service quality. It also ensures that Coupang's fixed investments in its logistics network is maximised.

While competitors rely on third-party logistics and fragmented seller performance, Coupang’s integrated model results in a predictable and delightful customer experience, providing a powerful moat.

This is also a similar strategy adopted by many of the market-leading E-commerce players around the world, giving them a distinct advantage over its competitors.

  • Amazon (AMZN) invested heavily in warehouses and its own last-mile fleet, making Prime a logistical promise, not just a loyalty program.

  • JD.com (JD) owns one of the most advanced logistics networks in China, enabling same-day delivery for much of its inventory.

  • MercadoLibre (MELI) in Latin America has poured resources into MercadoEnvios, giving it a structural advantage in regions with underdeveloped logistics.

The Coupang Flywheel: Ecosystem Advantage

Coupang’s strategy is not just about logistics — it’s about building a flywheel around the Wow membership, similar to Amazon Prime. As of the end of 2023, there were ~14 million WOW members in Korea. For a small monthly fee, users get:

  • Rocket Delivery: Free, fast delivery on millions of items.

  • Coupang Eats: A food delivery service where members get unlimited free delivery. Market share has doubled over the last year to ~35% at the expense of the market leader (Baedal Minjok).

  • Coupang Play: A streaming service with original and curated content. New content include LIV Golf, SNL Korea and even HBO content.

Each component supports the other: Coupang Play drives engagement and retention, Eats adds utility and frequency, and Rocket Delivery reinforces shopping behavior. Every additional Wow subscriber also means that Coupang can invest more (e.g. free delivery to Coupang Eats, more content for Coupang Play) by spreading the costs out to a wider customer base which then attracts more members. At the same time, the core commerce product is improving as selection gets wider with the turbo-charging of the 3P marketplace (see above).

This ecosystem is richer and more integrated than what competitors offer. Once you're in the eco-system, you won't leave, instead as the chart below shows, customers spend more.

Naver: Aggregator Strategy, Platform Weakness

Coupang's biggest competitor in Korea is Naver (i.e. Korea's Google) which has been heavily involved in E-commerce through its Naver Smart Stores platform, facilitating transactions between consumers and a multitude of third-party sellers.

Naver’s approach is more classic “platform economics.” It enables millions of merchants to sell online (3P marketplace). But it stops short of owning the full customer experience. Products sold, fulfillment, service quality, and delivery are left to third parties (e.g. CJ Logistics, Hanjin Logistics) — leading to inconsistency. They have also partnered with big brands and retailers (e.g. Emart) in an effort to increase supply.

Naver’s goal is to provide the platform that aggregates supply and demand, it’s a viable strategy for scale, but lacks control, which ultimately makes it hard to aggregate supply and demand. Consumers don’t know if delivery will be next-day or next-week, and sellers face uneven performance and higher abandonment risk. On the other hand, Coupang has built its ecosystem organically and to be more tightly integrated — it is certainly harder to execute and more expensive (Coupang has spent almost $5 billion in its logistics network), but more defensible in the long run.

This distinction matters: while Naver offers access, Coupang guarantees experience.

How long is the runway for growth?

Coupang's number of active commerce customers continues to grow, in the latest quarter (9% YoY growth), it's at 23.4 million customers. But when we consider South Korea's total population of approximately 51 million (& shrinking), the fact that Coupang already serves nearly half of the population suggests that future growth in new customer acquisition might become increasingly challenging. Furthermore, the low hanging fruits to be reaped in transitioning from offline to online sales may be slowing as online retail sales have already surpassed offline sales, accounting for 50.5% of total retail sales in 2023. In 2024, this trend continued with online sales surging by 15%, compared to the modest 2% growth in offline sales.

Despite these challenges I believe that growth will come by growing in:

  1. Rural/countryside areas of Korea;
  2. Spend per customer (discussed at length above); and
  3. Growing internationally.

Growing in the rural/countryside areas of Korea

This has long been an underserved segment, however, Coupang is leading the way in changing this. Coupang has plans to allocate over 3 trillion won (approximately $2.2 billion USD) by 2026, for at least eight new fulfillment centers strategically positioned throughout the country, with a specific focus on reaching island and mountainous regions. This is part of Coupang's efforts to offer its free Rocket Delivery to approximately 230 out of the 260 cities, counties, and districts nationwide by 2027, up from its current coverage of 182 cities, counties and district (as of Sep 2024). This also improves Coupang's moat relative to competitors who depend on 3rd party logistics who will not spend as much in expanding to these areas.

Online shopping and quick/fast delivery is undeniably a better option for customers in rural areas, many of whom are the elderly (see example).

Growing internationally in Taiwan

Taiwan has been Coupang's main area of focus outside of Korea. Coupang's strategy in Taiwan appears to be centered around bringing the "same jaw dropping wow experience" through a wide selection of products, competitive pricing, and fast, reliable delivery. They've spent more than $400 million trying to build out the logistics network in Taiwan and this has allowed it to launch its WOW membership which includes free shipping and free returns. In the most recent earnings call, management also said that they've expanded selection by nearly 500% this quarter by forging direct relationships with suppliers including global brands like Coke and P&G as well as local brands.

Yet, Shopee is the clear market leader and does not look like it has ceded any share (see below). Shopee (SE) is also well capitalised and has invested in its own logistics network (SPX Express), with most parcels reaching customers in 1-2 business days. Seemingly, Coupang's only value-add is in the large amount of Korean suppliers it has and the hope that its WOW membership (something Shopee does not have) is able to entice shoppers to join and remain in its eco-system. I remain skeptical that the product/experience is so much better relative to Shopee.

Does Farfetch add to the runway for growth or is it a distraction?

First, I want to clarify that for $500 million, the acquisition of Farfetch definitely seems cheap, with 2024 revenue of $1.7 billion and losses narrowing to $34 million. Yet, I believe that this is a huge distraction to Coupang and one that does not align with the strengths of Coupang, all while it is fighting many other battles.

Farfetch is a declining business (revenue down from $2.5 billion in 2023) and its turnaround efforts while successful financially, seems to be hastening the decline. This in-depth piece by the Business of Fashion is a fascinating look. Some quick points extracted out:

  • Coupang’s focus on driving efficiency... is hampering Farfetch’s relationships with customers... Last year, Farfetch cut teams of fashion advisors in London, New York, Hong Kong and Shanghai that worked exclusively with VIP customers, who represent about 30 percent of annual sales

  • Coupang also made logistics changes that saved millions of dollars but added friction for customers. In May, Farfetch switched its shipping provider from DHL and UPS to FedEx, according to three people familiar with the matter. The change has resulted in delays in delivery times for packages and refunds, one of the people said. Fast shipping is paramount for top shoppers who expect premium service. It’s also important for retailers to get returned items back into inventory quicker and avoid losing potential sales.

    “It’s very hard to regain a luxury customer after you’ve disappointed them,” Burke said. “They have other ways of purchasing products.”

  • Perhaps Farfetch’s most precarious relationship is with luxury brands, which along with independent retailers supply inventory to the site. Since Coupang's acquisition, Kering (Gucci, Saint Laurent, Bottega Veneta, etc), Celine and Alaia have cut ties with Farfetch as it has done away with guidelines that allow staffers to give top brands more control over how their products are sold on the marketplace.

Potential synergies also appear to be missing. There have also been no efforts to make Farfetch's supply of luxury products available on the main Coupang platform. But then again, it's unclear if this would drive significant incremental demand as it is unlikely that luxury shoppers will view Coupang as the platform to do their shopping where they can enjoy a luxurious VIP experience. In short, I am hoping that once the turnaround to profitability is done, Coupang flips Farfetch for a quick buck.

Valuation and Conclusion

Coupang’s Korea strategy—anchored by its fulfillment-led approach and the increasingly sticky Wow membership flywheel—is clearly working. I believe double-digit F/X-neutral growth should remain the base case moving forward for the core Product Commerce business. Any re-accelerating would also be evidence that Coupang's expansion into more rural regions is working and per customer spend is increasing as customers continue to choose Coupang. I’ve also been impressed by how far Coupang Eats and Coupang Play have come—both are strong testaments to the company’s operational execution. When Coupang has invested in Korea, it has delivered, and the ecosystem continues to compound. It is the clear leader in Korea and trades quite in-line with how the other E-commerce market leaders are doing.

That said, for this to be a home-run investment, it needs to be successful in its international expansion in Taiwan to truly expand the TAM. Most of the low-hanging fruits have been picked in Korea and to remain a hyper-growth company, growth must continue from outside. Yet, I remain skeptical about Taiwan and Farfetch. The Taiwan bet is bold and strategically adjacent, but doesn’t tie into the domestic flywheel it has built and it's definitely a large uphill climb (Coupang's management are great operators and executors but so is Shopee). Still, I’m willing to give management the benefit of the doubt—particularly because the execution bar in Korea has been so high. But with the scale of the investment, we’ll need more colour from management on progress. As for Farfetch, it’s hard to see the strategic fit or upside—my hope is they move quickly to sell it off and refocus resources on areas where Coupang’s model has proven it can win.

data.title: Global Medical REIT Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Global Medical REIT Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.
